input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring components such as a first vertical robot axis, permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, and cycloidal gear, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. This manufacturing robot operates in a precision motion control environment, where its mechanical dynamics and control system make it suitable for digital twinning to enhance motion accuracy and operational efficiency.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for multi-level abstraction modeling (requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers), RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for complexity management. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavior modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java for detailed system behavior implementation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system maintains real-time synchronization through automated one-way data flow from physical assets to the digital representation, with active components processing live data streams for immediate state updates.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved via Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardization, sensor data streaming using MQTT protocol, and connection to Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl industrial controllers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include structural components modeled via SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs), ACME, and AADL for component interfaces and interconnections; behavioral models using MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines; and analytical models implemented with MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ or Java. Data sources are integrated from heterogeneous stakeholders across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer) using MBSE methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical acting components consist of a permanent magnet synchronous motor driving the first axis, with a gear transmission followed by a cycloidal gear to achieve low gear ratios. Control is executed via the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, enabling precise actuation with defined safety constraints and remote interfaces for operational adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox. This arrangement reduces backlash and positional uncertainty, with data collected at high sampling frequencies and transmitted in real-time for precise state monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated through quantitative comparison of simulated data against measured data to assess deviations. Uncertainty sources such as mechanical backlash and positional inaccuracies are quantified using sensor data, with error correction mechanisms integrated into the control loop to maintain data consistency and model accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical to virtual include live measurements from the physical robot (e.g., sensor data, operational states) streamed via MQTT protocol as part of the one-way data flow between the physical object and the digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system utilizes industry-standard modeling languages and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as SysML [17], AADL [24], ACME [23], and MontiArc [25], [26] for structured representation across multiple abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). These standards support component & connector modeling, including interfaces and interconnections, and integrate with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink [28], general-purpose languages (C++, Java), and address heterogeneous stakeholder integration [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

It correctly identifies the operational domain as precision motion control for manufacturing. Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl.'

While it mentions outcomes like 'improve robot motion accuracy' and 'visualization in a dashboard', it does not explicitly define or categorize specific 'services' (e.g., monitoring, optimization) as a distinct functional offering of the Digital Twin in the way the characteristic description requires. Therefore, stating it is 'Not in Document' is accurate based on the provided evidence.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by Section II.A 'Modeling Software and Systems'. It accurately lists the tools and methodologies mentioned: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE)', 'SysML', 'RFLP', 'OOSEM', 'SPES', 'SMArDT', 'ACME', 'AADL', 'MontiArc', 'SysML IBDs', 'MontiArc Automaton', 'SysML v1.7 state machines', 'MathWorks Simulink', 'C++', and 'Java'. It correctly describes their functions, such as 'multi-level abstraction modeling' and 'describe components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition'. Supporting quotes include: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'methodologies... e.g., RFLP..., OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT'.

It does not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, data anonymization, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the assessment 'Not in Document' is correct.

While the source text mentions a physical 'safety cell' restricting the robot's working range, it lacks details on cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or risk assessment approaches as defined by the characteristic.
